batch_notes: Cleanup-and-gap pass over peptides previously populated in batch-01 (SS-31, MOTS-c, BPC-157, GHK-Cu) and batch-08 (Dihexa). Goals for this pass: (1) fill identity calculations left approximate or null in earlier batches; (2) add cytokine/interleukin rows that were missing or thin; (3) rewrite cascades to show true upstream→downstream flow rather than summary bullet lists; (4) surface contradictions and low-confidence claims explicitly with '?' symbols and safetyRisks entries; (5) tighten human vs. animal evidence separation. When merging into the site, batch-10 entries for these five peptides should be treated as the current-truth version and should supersede the corresponding batch-01 / batch-08 entries.

modifications: D-arginine at position 1 (protease resistance)
2',6'-dimethyl-L-tyrosine (Dmt) at position 2 — non-proteinogenic; critical for mitochondrial partitioning
L-lysine at position 3 — contributes to polycationic targeting
L-phenylalanine at position 4
C-terminal primary amide
Net charge +3 at physiologic pH; amphipathic aromatic-cationic motif underlies inner mitochondrial membrane accumulation
identity.structureClass: Aromatic-cationic tetrapeptide (Szeto-Schiller class); targets inner mitochondrial membrane without a classical mitochondrial targeting sequence

tile.mechanismSummary: Partitions 1000–5000× into the inner mitochondrial membrane via its aromatic-cationic motif, binds cardiolipin, and stabilizes ETC supercomplex architecture — reducing electron leak, mitochondrial ROS, and cristae disorganization in dysfunctional mitochondria.
clinicalUses: Primary mitochondrial myopathy — MMPOWER-3 Phase 3 did not meet primary endpoints
Barth syndrome — TAZPOWER mixed with subsequent open-label improvements; FDA review trajectory has been contested and should be re-verified
sideEffects: Injection-site reactions (dominant AE across trials — frequently >30% of participants)

dosing.quick: Investigational only. Trial-era adult dosing has been 40 mg subcutaneous once daily in Barth syndrome and primary mitochondrial myopathy. Not an approved product; not a validated dose outside trial settings.
dosing.adminRoute: Subcutaneous injection (also IV in some studies; ophthalmic formulation studied for AMD)

proteins: Cardiolipin — direct phospholipid binding target (not a protein, but the canonical molecular partner)
Cytochrome c — cardiolipin-bound pool; interaction with cardiolipin-elamipretide complex reduces cytochrome c peroxidase activity
ETC complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase)
ETC complex III (cytochrome bc1)
ETC complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase)
Supercomplex I+III2+IV (respirasome) — assembly preserved under elamipretide
ATP synthase (complex V) — output preserved indirectly via supercomplex stabilization
OPA1 — inner membrane dynamin involved in cristae morphology; not a direct binding partner but downstream of membrane lipid organization
channelsTransporters: Mitochondrial permeability transition pore (MPTP) — opening probability reduced indirectly via preserved cristae organization and reduced matrix Ca2+ overload (indirect, not direct binding)

cytokinesInterleukins.name: IL-6
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Reduced in some ischemia and heart-failure rodent models; indirect via mitochondrial ROS reduction rather than direct cytokine modulation
cytokinesInterleukins.context: Preclinical; mechanism is downstream of reduced ROS-driven NF-κB activity

cytokinesInterleukins.name: TNF-α
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Reduced in rodent ischemia-reperfusion; indirect via ROS/NF-κB dampening
cytokinesInterleukins.context: Preclinical; no human cytokine endpoint data at this level of specificity

steps: Elamipretide crosses outer mitochondrial membrane (passive, charge-independent)
Polycationic motif drives electrostatic accumulation against the inner-membrane potential (ΔΨm)
Partitioning 1000–5000× into the IMM inner leaflet cardiolipin domains
Binding to cardiolipin (non-receptor, lipid-peptide interaction)
Stabilization of cardiolipin-cytochrome c interaction
Preservation of ETC supercomplex (I+III2+IV) architecture
Reduction in electron leak from ETC complexes I and III
Reduction in mitochondrial superoxide and H2O2 output
Preservation of cristae curvature and ATP synthase organization
Net: restored oxidative phosphorylation efficiency in dysfunctional mitochondria
Cardiolipin binding reduces cytochrome c peroxidase activity
Reduced cardiolipin peroxidation preserves cytochrome c retention at the IMM
Reduced apoptotic cytochrome c release into cytosol
Reduced caspase-9 activation in injury models
This pathway is well-supported in cell and rodent ischemia data; not directly measured in human trials
Mechanism produces robust in vitro and rodent benefit
Short-term human biomarkers (e.g., 6-minute walk test improvements in early trials) support clinical relevance
Pivotal MMPOWER-3 in primary mitochondrial myopathy did not meet 6MWT or symptom primary endpoints

TAZPOWER in Barth syndrome was negative in randomized phase but positive in open-label extension — a pattern that is not consistent with high placebo effect and suggests either real delayed benefit or selection bias in OLE
Translation from mechanism to reliable clinical endpoint benefit in humans is genuinely contested

expanded.humanEvidence: Human evidence is substantial but the results are heterogeneous. Acute-dose tolerability and PK have been established in multiple Phase 1 and Phase 2 studies. MMPOWER-3 (NCT03323749) in primary mitochondrial myopathy did not meet its primary efficacy endpoints on 6-minute walk test distance or the PMM Symptom Assessment Total Fatigue score. TAZPOWER (NCT03098797) in Barth syndrome was negative in its randomized phase but reported functional improvements during open-label extension. Stealth Biotherapeutics pursued Barth syndrome approval on the basis of natural-history-controlled OLE data; FDA review has involved multiple cycles including a Complete Response Letter and advisory committee activity, and the current regulatory status should be verified against the most recent FDA communications before the site goes live. ReCLAIM and related ophthalmic studies have explored geographic atrophy with modest functional signals.
expanded.animalEvidence: Consistent across cardiac ischemia-reperfusion, heart failure, renal ischemia, sarcopenia, age-related mitochondrial decline, and neurodegeneration models. Elamipretide restores cristae structure, reduces mitochondrial ROS, and improves ATP production in aged or injured tissues. Independent replication by unaffiliated laboratories is good for the core mechanism claims.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Elamipretide is an aromatic-cationic tetrapeptide that accumulates 1000–5000× in the inner mitochondrial membrane via an electrostatic and hydrophobic mechanism, without a classical mitochondrial targeting sequence. Its canonical molecular partner is cardiolipin — not a protein receptor but the signature phospholipid of the inner mitochondrial membrane. Cardiolipin binding stabilizes cytochrome c-cardiolipin interactions (reducing peroxidase activity and apoptotic release), preserves the assembly of electron transport chain supercomplexes (I+III2+IV), and supports cristae curvature. Downstream effects include reduced electron leak, reduced mitochondrial ROS, improved oxidative phosphorylation efficiency, and preserved ATP output. Elamipretide does not scavenge ROS in a stoichiometric antioxidant sense — its effect is architectural (maintaining the membrane organization that prevents ROS generation), not catalytic. The translation gap from this well-supported mechanism to reliable human clinical endpoint benefit is the central unresolved question.
expanded.safetyDetail: Human safety data are reasonably well characterized for subcutaneous use in adults with mitochondrial disease over months to years. Injection-site reactions are the dominant adverse event (frequently >30% of participants, occasionally causing discontinuation). Systemic AEs reported include headache, nausea, dizziness. No clear hepatotoxicity or cardiac safety signal has emerged. Long-term safety beyond several years is not established. Safety in healthy non-diseased adults is not established — no placebo-controlled healthy-volunteer long-term dosing data exist.

safetyRisks.label: Pivotal trial did not meet primary endpoints
safetyRisks.harmSummary: MMPOWER-3 was negative on its primary efficacy endpoints. Mechanistic plausibility does not equal demonstrated clinical benefit for primary mitochondrial myopathy.

safetyRisks.label: Not FDA-approved; Barth syndrome trajectory in flux
safetyRisks.harmSummary: No approved product. Barth syndrome review has involved a Complete Response Letter and ongoing activity; re-verify status against current FDA communications before publication.

enhancementPotential.summary: Enhancement claims in healthy adults (anti-aging, athletic mitochondrial support) are not supported by controlled human evidence. All positive human readouts are in diseased populations with baseline mitochondrial dysfunction.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Do not infer healthy-adult benefit from Barth syndrome, PMM, or aged-rodent data. The architectural (non-stoichiometric) mechanism means the ceiling of benefit is bounded by the degree of pre-existing mitochondrial dysfunction — there may be little to 'fix' in healthy adults.

missingEvidence: Placebo-controlled human trials in healthy adults
Long-term (>5 year) safety data
Pharmacokinetics and biomarker response in non-diseased populations
Independent mechanistic replication of specific ETC supercomplex stabilization claim in human tissue
anecdotalUse: Gray-market use for 'mitochondrial anti-aging' — unsupported by controlled human data
Compounded injectable use marketed to athletes — no clinical trial support and regulatory status unclear

claims.value: Elamipretide binds cardiolipin on the inner mitochondrial membrane and stabilizes ETC supercomplex (I+III2+IV) assembly, reducing electron leak and mitochondrial ROS in dysfunctional mitochondria.
claims.population: cell-free assays, cell culture, rodent ischemia and aging models

claims.value: Cardiolipin binding reduces cytochrome c peroxidase activity and attenuates mitochondrial apoptotic release in injury models.
claims.population: cell culture and rodent ischemia models

claims.value: Translation from well-supported mechanism to reliable clinical endpoint benefit in humans is genuinely contested — the pivotal MMPOWER-3 trial was negative while TAZPOWER showed open-label-phase improvements that are mechanistically plausible but methodologically weaker.
claims.population: primary mitochondrial myopathy and Barth syndrome patients

claims.value: Not FDA-approved as of knowledge cutoff; Barth syndrome review has been active and re-verification is required before publication.

modifications: No post-translational modifications described for the canonical MOTS-c peptide
Encoded within the mitochondrial 12S rRNA gene (MT-RNR1) via an alternative open reading frame
Translation occurs in mitochondria; peptide is then exported to cytosol and, under stress, to nucleus
identity.structureClass: Mitochondrial-derived peptide (MDP); small ORF product encoded within MT-RNR1

classification.mechanismFamily: AMPK pathway activator / metabolic stress signaling peptide

tile.mechanismSummary: A 16-residue peptide encoded within the mitochondrial 12S rRNA that activates AMPK signaling and, under metabolic stress, translocates to the nucleus to regulate antioxidant and metabolic gene expression. Exerts metformin-like effects on glucose and lipid metabolism in rodents. No canonical receptor has been identified.
tile.localization: Mitochondria (origin) → cytosol → nucleus (under stress); plasma concentration declines with age
sideEffects: Human safety is not characterized — no published human dosing trials of exogenous MOTS-c

dosing.quick: No human dosing regimen exists. Rodent studies typically use 0.1–5 mg/kg intraperitoneal or subcutaneous. Do not extrapolate to human use.
dosing.adminRoute: Intraperitoneal or subcutaneous in rodent studies; no validated human route

genes: MT-RNR1 (12S rRNA gene; contains the MOTS-c ORF)
proteins: AMPK — principal downstream kinase activated (not a direct binding target; activation is indirect via folate/methionine cycle interference and AICAR accumulation)
ACC (acetyl-CoA carboxylase) — phosphorylated downstream of AMPK; inhibition of lipogenesis
TFEB — nuclear translocation partner under stress (reported)
NRF2 — oxidative stress response; MOTS-c-nuclear translocation is proposed to engage this axis
ATF1 — nuclear target under glucose restriction (reported)
FOXO3 — potentially modulated downstream of AMPK
receptors: No canonical cell-surface receptor has been identified for exogenous MOTS-c — this is an important mechanistic gap that affects interpretation of all gray-market dosing claims
channelsTransporters: GLUT4 — membrane translocation increased in skeletal muscle in rodent studies (indirect via AMPK)

cytokinesInterleukins.name: IL-6
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Reduced in rodent diet-induced obesity and aging models
cytokinesInterleukins.context: Preclinical; indirect via AMPK-mediated NF-κB suppression

cytokinesInterleukins.name: TNF-α
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Reduced in some rodent metabolic inflammation models

cytokinesInterleukins.name: IL-10
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Modulated in inflammation models; direction is context-dependent

steps: MOTS-c is translated from the 12S rRNA ORF within mitochondria
Exported to cytosol (transport mechanism not fully defined)
Interferes with folate-methionine one-carbon cycle intermediates
AICAR (5-aminoimidazole-4-carboxamide ribonucleotide) accumulates
AMPK activation (AICAR is a classical AMPK activator; this mechanism parallels metformin's proposed AMPK engagement)
AMPK phosphorylates ACC → inhibits lipogenesis
AMPK promotes fatty acid oxidation and GLUT4 translocation in skeletal muscle
Net metabolic phenotype in rodents: improved insulin sensitivity, reduced diet-induced obesity
Metabolic stress (glucose restriction, oxidative stress, exercise)
MOTS-c accumulates in nucleus
Engages stress-responsive transcription factors (NRF2-related, ATF1)
Regulates antioxidant and metabolic gene expression
Contributes to adaptive mitochondrial-nuclear communication — the MOTS-c retrograde signaling hypothesis
No cell-surface receptor identified for exogenous MOTS-c
Endogenous peptide's transport across mitochondrial, plasma, and nuclear membranes is not mechanistically defined
Exogenous subcutaneous administration assumes cellular uptake and intracellular availability that have not been demonstrated at therapeutic concentrations in humans
This gap is a methodological obstacle to translating endogenous MOTS-c biology into exogenous dosing claims

expanded.humanEvidence: Observational human studies have measured endogenous circulating MOTS-c and reported inverse associations with obesity, insulin resistance, and some aging markers; circulating MOTS-c declines with age. These are correlative and cannot support exogenous dosing efficacy claims. There are no published randomized controlled trials of exogenous administered MOTS-c in humans as of the knowledge cutoff. The absence of any human efficacy data is the single most important fact about MOTS-c for a peptide reference site.
expanded.animalEvidence: In rodents, MOTS-c administration improves insulin sensitivity, protects against diet-induced obesity, enhances exercise capacity, and reduces age-related metabolic decline. Effects are attributed to AMPK activation and mitochondrial adaptation. Evidence is reasonably consistent but much of it originates from the original discovering group and close collaborators; independent replication is still accumulating.
expanded.mechanismDetail: MOTS-c is one of several mitochondrial-derived peptides translated from small ORFs in mitochondrial RNA (others include humanin and the SHLPs). Its proposed dominant mechanism is indirect activation of AMPK via interference with folate-methionine one-carbon metabolism and AICAR accumulation — the same general axis that metformin is thought to engage. Under metabolic stress MOTS-c translocates to the nucleus and engages stress-responsive transcription, in particular through NRF2-related antioxidant gene expression. The important mechanistic caveat is that no canonical cell-surface receptor has been identified for exogenous MOTS-c, and the mechanism by which subcutaneously administered MOTS-c would reproduce the effects of the endogenous peptide (which is translated and acts intracellularly) is not established.
expanded.safetyDetail: No human safety data. Rodent studies at doses up to several mg/kg have not reported major toxicity, but long-term safety, immunogenicity, and off-target effects in humans are unknown. AMPK activation has tissue-specific and dose-dependent effects that are not fully characterized at therapeutic doses in humans — an AMPK activator could plausibly have both beneficial and problematic effects depending on tissue context (e.g., beneficial in metabolic tissues, potentially problematic in cardiac or regenerative contexts).

safetyRisks.label: No human safety characterization
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Endogenous peptide with broad metabolic effects.
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Exogenous dosing in humans is untested at any duration. AMPK activation has tissue-specific effects not validated at therapeutic doses in humans.

safetyRisks.label: No identified receptor for exogenous peptide
safetyRisks.harmSummary: The mechanistic bridge from 'circulating MOTS-c correlates with metabolic health' to 'injecting MOTS-c produces metabolic benefit' has not been established at the molecular level.

safetyRisks.label: Gray-market supply chain
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Research-grade or online material lacks identity, purity, and sterility assurance.

enhancementPotential.summary: Enhancement claims for weight loss, longevity, and athletic performance in healthy humans are not supported by human trial evidence. Rodent and correlational human data are suggestive but not dispositive. The absence of a defined receptor is a specific mechanistic problem for exogenous dosing claims.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Correlation of endogenous circulating MOTS-c with metabolic health does not imply that exogenous administration will reproduce those effects in humans, particularly given the unresolved receptor / uptake biology.

missingEvidence: Any randomized controlled human trial of administered MOTS-c
Identified cell-surface receptor or primary binding partner for exogenous peptide
Pharmacokinetics and bioavailability in humans
Long-term preclinical safety / carcinogenicity
Independent (non-Lee-group) replication of key rodent metabolic findings
anecdotalUse: Gray-market use as a 'mitochondrial biogenesis' or 'metabolic longevity' injectable — no human clinical evidence supports this use

claims.value: MOTS-c activates AMPK indirectly via folate/methionine cycle interference and AICAR accumulation, improving insulin sensitivity in rodent diet-induced obesity models.
Under metabolic stress MOTS-c translocates to the nucleus and engages stress-responsive transcription (NRF2 axis, ATF1).
claims.population: cultured mammalian cells and mouse tissues

claims.value: No canonical cell-surface receptor has been identified for exogenous MOTS-c; this is a specific mechanistic gap that affects interpretation of subcutaneous dosing claims.
No randomized controlled trials of administered MOTS-c in humans have been published.

modifications: Synthetic; corresponds to a 15-aa fragment originally described from a region of human gastric juice protein
Free N- and C-termini in the canonical preparation
Stable across pH range tested in gastric juice conditions

classification.peptideClass: Gastric juice-derived synthetic peptide (cytoprotective / pro-angiogenic candidate)
classification.mechanismFamily: NO pathway and VEGFR2-associated pro-angiogenic signaling (proposed, not conclusively established)

tile.mechanismSummary: A 15-amino-acid synthetic peptide promoted as a broad tissue-healing agent. Proposed mechanisms invoke the NO-NOS system, VEGFR2-mediated angiogenesis, and growth hormone receptor upregulation in fibroblasts. Mechanism claims are drawn almost entirely from rodent studies by a single research lineage; independent replication and receptor-binding data are limited.
tile.localization: Systemic in preclinical studies; reported tissue effects in gut, tendon, ligament, muscle, and CNS models
sideEffects: Human adverse event profile is poorly characterized — no adequately powered human trials
FDA has flagged compounding safety and identity concerns

dosing.quick: No approved human dose. Rodent studies commonly use 10 µg/kg IP or PO. Gray-market subcutaneous protocols of 250–500 µg daily are not supported by published human trial data.
dosing.adminRoute: Intraperitoneal, subcutaneous, or oral in rodent studies; no validated human route or dose

proteins: VEGFR2 (KDR) — proposed interaction in pro-angiogenic effects; binding data weak
eNOS (NOS3) — upregulated in several rodent models; mechanism is transcriptional rather than direct binding
nNOS (NOS1) — modulated in CNS models
Growth hormone receptor — upregulation reported in tendon fibroblasts; not a direct binding target
EGR-1 (early growth response 1) — induction reported
FAK, paxillin — cytoskeletal signaling reported downstream
5-HT (serotonin) system — interactions reported in CNS models
Dopamine system — interactions reported in CNS models
receptors: No canonical receptor has been identified with published high-quality binding data
All proposed receptor interactions (VEGFR2, GHR) are inferred from downstream pathway modulation and pharmacological blockade, not direct binding

steps: BPC-157 administration (local or systemic)
Upregulation of VEGF expression in injured tissue
Proposed VEGFR2 signaling activation (direct binding not established)
Increased eNOS expression and NO bioavailability
Enhanced endothelial cell migration and granulation tissue formation
Faster closure of experimental wounds and soft-tissue repair in rodents
BPC-157 exposure to tenocytes / fibroblasts
Upregulation of growth hormone receptor expression
Enhanced responsiveness to circulating GH
Increased collagen synthesis and tenocyte migration
Improved tendon healing in rodent models
This axis is inferred from downstream changes rather than direct binding experiments
Rodent effects are reported across an unusually wide range of tissues and injury types
Nearly all positive findings originate from a single laboratory lineage (Sikiric group) or close collaborators
Independent replication by unaffiliated groups is limited — the Chang 2011 paper is one of the few exceptions and its scope is narrower than the broader claims
The pattern of 'broadly effective with no failed indications' is not consistent with how most pharmacological agents behave and is a methodological red flag
Angiogenesis mechanism invoked for healing is also a shared feature of tumor growth — the promotional literature does not adequately address oncological risk

expanded.humanEvidence: There are no adequately powered, well-published randomized controlled trials of BPC-157 in humans as of the knowledge cutoff. Early clinical activity exists for a related formulation (PL 14736) in inflammatory bowel disease from the early 2000s, but no Phase 3 human data support the contemporary gray-market musculoskeletal and gastrointestinal uses. Human safety data are sparse. The FDA's 503A compounding review has flagged identity and safety concerns. WADA has prohibited BPC-157 since January 2022.
expanded.animalEvidence: Extensive rodent literature across gut ulcer, colitis, tendon, ligament, muscle, bone, skin, vascular, and CNS injury models. Effects are broad and unusually consistent — a pattern that can reflect a genuinely pleiotropic agent but that is also consistent with publication bias, lab-specific methodology, or an overly inclusive model panel. The single-lineage nature of most of this literature is a substantial methodological caveat that should be surfaced to readers.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Proposed mechanisms center on the NO-NOS system, VEGFR2-driven angiogenesis, and upregulation of growth hormone receptor signaling in fibroblasts and tenocytes. BPC-157 does not have a confirmed canonical receptor with published high-quality binding data; proposed receptor interactions are inferred from downstream pathway changes and pharmacological blockade experiments. The absence of direct binding studies is a mechanistic gap that the site should acknowledge even when describing the rodent efficacy literature.
expanded.safetyDetail: In rodents, BPC-157 appears to have a wide therapeutic index and no notable toxicity at tested doses. Human safety evidence is limited to small early studies of related formulations. Long-term human safety, immunogenicity, and oncological risk are unknown. The pro-angiogenic mechanism invoked for healing is a theoretical oncological concern that the promotional literature does not address. FDA 503A compounding concerns and WADA prohibition are concrete regulatory signals.

safetyRisks.label: Not approved; flagged for 503A compounding safety
safetyRisks.harmSummary: FDA has identified BPC-157 as a substance with insufficient safety information for 503A compounding use. WADA prohibits at all times.

safetyRisks.label: Gray-market identity and purity
safetyRisks.harmSummary: No guarantee of peptide identity, purity, sterility, or endotoxin control.

safetyRisks.label: Pro-angiogenic mechanism — tumor concern
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Angiogenesis is shared between wound healing and tumor biology.
safetyRisks.harmSummary: No human data on chronic systemic dosing in occult malignancy. Promotional literature does not address this.

safetyRisks.label: Single-lineage dominance of primary literature
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Most rodent efficacy data come from one research lineage.
safetyRisks.harmSummary: The breadth and consistency of reported effects across disparate injury models is a methodological red flag that warrants independent replication by unaffiliated groups.

enhancementPotential.summary: Popular claims for accelerated tendon, ligament, muscle, and gut healing in humans are not supported by adequate human RCT evidence.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Rodent wound-healing improvement — especially from a single research lineage — is not evidence of human efficacy. The 'broadly effective with no failed indications' pattern is itself suspicious.

missingEvidence: Adequately powered randomized controlled human trials for any indication
Independent replication by unaffiliated laboratories of key rodent findings
Published high-quality receptor binding data for any proposed target
Long-term human safety and oncology surveillance data
anecdotalUse: Gastrointestinal symptom management (leaky gut, IBD-like symptoms)
Post-surgical recovery and exercise-related soft-tissue injury

claims.value: BPC-157 promotes angiogenesis and tissue repair in rodent injury models via NO and VEGF upregulation; direct VEGFR2 binding has not been established with high-quality data.
claims.population: rats, mice in tendon/muscle/gut injury models

claims.value: BPC-157 upregulates growth hormone receptor expression in rodent tenocytes and fibroblasts; mechanism is inferred from downstream changes rather than direct binding.
The breadth, consistency, and single-lineage origin of rodent efficacy reports for BPC-157 is a methodological red flag that warrants independent replication by unaffiliated groups.
No adequately powered published randomized human trials support modern BPC-157 use in musculoskeletal or gastrointestinal indications.
BPC-157 is not FDA-approved; FDA has flagged it for 503A compounding safety concerns, and WADA has prohibited it since January 2022.

tradeNames: widely used as 'copper peptide' in cosmetic products

identity.formula: C14H24N6O4 (free GHK peptide, apo form); C14H22CuN6O4 (1:1 Cu(II) complex, with one peptide proton displaced on copper coordination)
modifications: Free GHK peptide molecular weight: 340.38 Da (C14H24N6O4) — this is the apo-peptide without copper
GHK-Cu 1:1 Cu(II) complex molecular weight: 402.9 Da — biologically active form
Copper coordination: N-terminal glycine amino group, histidine imidazole N, and a deprotonated backbone amide nitrogen
The copper-bound form is the pharmacologically active species; apo-GHK has weaker biological activity

classification.mechanismFamily: Copper delivery / extracellular matrix remodeling / transcriptional modulator

tile.mechanismSummary: An endogenous tripeptide that binds Cu(II) with high affinity and modulates extracellular matrix remodeling, antioxidant response, and angiogenesis in skin. Widely used as a cosmetic ingredient; limited small clinical trials support topical skin benefits. Systemic therapeutic claims are poorly supported.
tile.localization: Extracellular and intracellular in skin and connective tissue; human plasma levels decline with age (~200 ng/mL in youth to ~80 ng/mL by age 60)
clinicalUses: Cosmetic topical use for skin aging and wound support (widespread ingredient use)
Investigational topical use for wound healing
sideEffects: Topical: generally well tolerated; occasional contact dermatitis
Injectable use is not a standard clinical practice and is not well characterized in humans

dosing.quick: Topical cosmetic use at ~0.05–0.2% is typical in published skin studies. No validated systemic dose. Injectable gray-market use is not supported by controlled human trial data.
dosing.adminRoute: Topical (cosmetic, wound-care); injectable in unregulated gray-market use; IV historically in early wound-healing research

proteins: Collagen (type I and III) — synthesis stimulated in dermal fibroblasts
Elastin — synthesis stimulated in some models
Decorin — induced
Fibronectin — induced
MMP-2 — context-dependent modulation (not simple inhibition)
TGF-β1 — modulated in skin models
VEGF — increased in wound-healing and vascular models
SOD1 (Cu,Zn-SOD) — activity supported via copper delivery
Lysyl oxidase (LOX) — copper-dependent enzyme supported by GHK-mediated copper delivery
Cytochrome c oxidase — copper-dependent; indirectly supported
receptors: No classical receptor — GHK-Cu works primarily as a copper carrier and transcriptional modulator via intracellular mechanisms
channelsTransporters: CTR1 (SLC31A1) — cellular copper uptake transporter; receives copper from GHK
ATP7A / ATP7B — copper efflux / loading; downstream of cellular copper handling

cytokinesInterleukins.name: TGF-β1
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Modulated (often increased) in dermal fibroblast models
cytokinesInterleukins.context: Cell and small human skin studies; central to collagen synthesis signaling

cytokinesInterleukins.name: TNF-α
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Reduced in some inflammation / wound models

cytokinesInterleukins.name: IL-6
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Reduced in some skin inflammation models

cytokinesInterleukins.name: VEGF
cytokinesInterleukins.effect: Increased in wound-healing and vascular models
cytokinesInterleukins.context: Preclinical and limited human; central to wound-healing story

steps: GHK-Cu penetrates stratum corneum (topical) or reaches dermal fibroblasts (systemic)
Copper transferred to cellular copper handling machinery (including CTR1-mediated uptake)
Cu(II) delivered to copper-dependent enzymes: lysyl oxidase (for collagen cross-linking), Cu,Zn-SOD (antioxidant), cytochrome c oxidase (mitochondrial)
Net effect: supports copper-dependent processes in skin and connective tissue
Topical GHK-Cu exposure of dermal fibroblasts
Upregulation of collagen I/III, elastin, decorin, fibronectin synthesis
Modulation of MMP-2 activity (context-dependent)
TGF-β1 modulation contributes to collagen synthesis signaling
Observable human endpoints: improved skin firmness, density, fine wrinkle reduction in small topical trials
GHK exposure of cultured cells (Pickart-group microarray studies)
Reported modulation of hundreds to thousands of genes spanning tissue remodeling, antioxidant defense, and DNA repair
This breadth of transcriptional effect is striking and has not been robustly independently replicated
The magnitude of the transcriptomic claim exceeds what the primary copper-delivery mechanism would predict and warrants skepticism until replicated

expanded.humanEvidence: Small controlled and open-label human studies — primarily in cosmetics and wound-care — support topical GHK-Cu effects on skin firmness, fine wrinkles, photodamage markers, and wound healing. Studies are generally small (n=20-100), often industry-sponsored, and use varied formulations. There are no large randomized trials of systemic (injected) GHK-Cu in humans for any indication; the robust human evidence is confined to topical cosmetic-scale endpoints. Historical FDA pharmaceutical approvals exist for prezatide copper acetate (early 1990s) for wound applications, but these have not translated to current marketed products.
expanded.animalEvidence: Rodent studies support roles in wound healing, hair follicle stimulation, and tissue repair. Gene expression studies in cell lines document broad transcriptional effects consistent with wound-repair programs, though much of this work originates from the Pickart lineage.
expanded.mechanismDetail: GHK is an endogenous tripeptide present in human plasma at nanomolar concentrations, declining with age (from ~200 ng/mL in youth to ~80 ng/mL by age 60). It binds Cu(II) with high affinity via N-terminal amino group, histidine imidazole, and a deprotonated backbone amide — acting as a copper shuttle between plasma proteins (notably albumin) and cells. Once copper is delivered, it supports copper-dependent enzymes including lysyl oxidase (critical for collagen cross-linking), Cu,Zn-SOD (antioxidant defense), and cytochrome c oxidase (mitochondrial respiration). The ECM-remodeling effect in dermal fibroblasts (upregulation of collagen I/III, elastin, decorin, fibronectin; modulation of MMP-2) is the best-supported part of the mechanism. The broad transcriptomic effects reported in microarray studies are striking but primarily single-source and exceed what the copper-delivery mechanism alone would predict.
expanded.safetyDetail: Topical GHK-Cu has a strong cosmetic-scale safety record over decades as a cosmetic ingredient. Contact dermatitis is uncommon. Systemic or injected use is not standard and lacks modern controlled safety data. Copper overload is theoretically possible with chronic high-dose injection, particularly in individuals with Wilson disease or copper-handling disorders.

safetyRisks.label: Contact dermatitis
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Occasional local irritation or allergic contact dermatitis to topical products.

safetyRisks.label: Copper overload (theoretical)
safetyRisks.helpSummary: Copper-related hepatic/neurologic risk in susceptible individuals.
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Contraindicated in Wilson disease or impaired biliary copper excretion. Chronic parenteral copper peptide dosing is not established as safe.

safetyRisks.label: Transcriptomic claims from single research lineage
safetyRisks.harmSummary: The breadth of reported transcriptional effects is primarily from the Pickart group and has not been robustly replicated at the claimed magnitude by unaffiliated laboratories.

enhancementPotential.summary: Topical cosmetic benefits (skin firmness, wrinkle reduction, wound healing support) have modest but real human evidence. Systemic anti-aging, hair regrowth, and healing claims outside topical use are not supported by controlled human trials.
enhancementPotential.caveat: Do not generalize topical cosmetic trial outcomes to systemic injectable use or to longevity endpoints.

missingEvidence: Large randomized trials of systemic / injectable GHK-Cu for any therapeutic indication
Independent (non-Pickart-group) replication of broad transcriptomic claims
Long-term safety of chronic parenteral use
Head-to-head comparison against established dermatologic ingredients (retinoids, peptide complexes) in photoaging
anecdotalUse: Topical cosmetic use for anti-aging and post-procedure skin recovery
Scalp application for hair density (weak human evidence)
Gray-market systemic injection for 'regenerative' effects (not supported by controlled human trials)

claims.value: GHK binds Cu(II) with high affinity and functions as a copper shuttle, delivering copper to copper-dependent enzymes including lysyl oxidase, Cu,Zn-SOD, and cytochrome c oxidase.
Topical GHK-Cu improves measured skin parameters (firmness, density, fine wrinkles, photodamage markers) in small human studies.

GHK is reported to modulate expression of hundreds to thousands of genes across tissue remodeling, antioxidant defense, and DNA repair pathways; the magnitude of this transcriptomic claim exceeds the copper-delivery mechanism and has not been robustly independently replicated.

citations.title: Regenerative and protective actions of the GHK-Cu peptide in the light of the new gene data
citations.notes: Int J Mol Sci 19(7):1987. Pickart is the discoverer; much of the transcriptomic literature comes from his group. Treat as interested-party summary for magnitude claims.

citations.title: Skin care benefits of copper peptide containing facial cream
citations.notes: AAD meeting abstract / paper on topical copper peptide facial cream. Specific bibliographic record should be verified before publication.

modifications: N-terminal hexanoyl cap (C6 fatty acid) — improves oral bioavailability and CNS penetration
C-terminal 6-aminohexanoic acid amide (Aha-NH2) — linker/spacer with terminal primary amide
Only Tyr-Ile is a canonical dipeptide; dihexa is better described as a peptidomimetic than a classical peptide
Derived from angiotensin IV (Ang IV) by the Harding laboratory at Washington State University
Molecular formula and mass now calculated explicitly in this cleanup pass: hexanoyl (C6H11O) + Tyr residue (C9H9NO2) + Ile residue (C6H11NO) + -NH-(CH2)5-CONH2 (C6H13N2O) = C27H44N4O5, MW 504.67
identity.structureClass: Angiotensin IV-derived peptidomimetic; proposed HGF / c-Met pathway potentiator

tile.mechanismSummary: An orally bioavailable peptidomimetic derived from angiotensin IV, developed at the Harding lab for neurological applications. Proposed to augment HGF / c-Met signaling to promote synaptogenesis and dendritic spine formation. Spectacular preclinical potency claims warrant independent replication before clinical translation.
tile.localization: CNS (claimed BBB penetration); c-Met pathway is broadly expressed
sideEffects: No human data — safety profile in humans is unknown

dosing.quick: No human dose. Rat behavioral studies used oral doses in the high µg/kg range. No validated human dosing exists.
dosing.adminRoute: Oral in preclinical work (the BBB-penetrant design rationale); subcutaneous in some gray-market use

proteins: Hepatocyte growth factor (HGF) — dihexa is proposed to augment HGF signaling rather than bind c-Met directly
c-Met (MET) — receptor tyrosine kinase downstream of HGF
PI3K — downstream of c-Met
AKT (PKB) — downstream of PI3K/c-Met
ERK (MAPK) — downstream of c-Met
Angiotensin IV (parent system) — dihexa is derived from Ang IV but its proposed dominant mechanism is through HGF/c-Met rather than AT4 receptor
BDNF — downstream induction reported in some preparations
receptors: c-Met (MET) — indirect engagement via HGF augmentation (not direct binding)
AT4 receptor / IRAP (insulin-regulated aminopeptidase) — parent Ang IV target; relevance to dihexa is unclear

steps: Dihexa administration (oral in rodent studies; penetrates BBB)
Proposed augmentation of HGF binding or presentation to c-Met (direct binding to either HGF or c-Met not convincingly demonstrated)
c-Met receptor tyrosine kinase activation
Downstream PI3K/AKT and MAPK/ERK signaling
Increased dendritic spine density in cultured hippocampal neurons
Behavioral improvements in scopolamine-treated and aged rodent memory models
Magnitude of reported potency has not been robustly independently replicated
Reported synaptogenic potency: 'orders of magnitude greater than BDNF' in some assays — an extraordinary claim
Most dihexa-specific characterization originates from the Harding lab and close collaborators
Independent replication at the claimed potency magnitude by unaffiliated groups is limited
c-Met is a known oncogene — chronic pathway engagement in healthy adults is a genuine safety concern not addressed by preclinical cognitive benefit data
Gray-market molecular identity is frequently unverified — buyers rarely confirm that the compound matches the Harding-lab structure

expanded.humanEvidence: There are no published human clinical trials of dihexa as of the knowledge cutoff. All efficacy claims are preclinical. The compound has been discussed in the Alzheimer's drug-development literature as a potential candidate, but no IND-enabling human data are publicly available.
expanded.animalEvidence: Rat studies from the Harding lab report behavioral improvements (Morris water maze, object recognition) in aged and scopolamine-treated models, and dendritic spine formation in cultured hippocampal neurons. Reported potency in synaptogenesis assays is very high — described as orders of magnitude greater than BDNF in some preparations. This potency claim has not been robustly replicated by independent laboratories at the originally reported magnitude, which is a methodological concern given the scale of the claim.
expanded.mechanismDetail: Dihexa is derived from angiotensin IV (Ang IV) by the Harding group at Washington State University. Structurally it is a small peptidomimetic with a hexanoyl cap, a Tyr-Ile dipeptide core, and a 6-aminohexanoic acid amide tail — designed for oral absorption and CNS penetration (confirmed molecular formula: C27H44N4O5, MW 504.67). The proposed mechanism of action is not direct c-Met binding but rather augmentation of HGF-mediated c-Met signaling, possibly by stabilizing or presenting HGF at c-Met. Downstream effects include PI3K/AKT and MAPK/ERK activation, dendritic spine formation, and improved memory performance in rodent models. The relationship to the parent Ang IV / AT4 receptor / IRAP system is complex and not fully resolved; dihexa's dominant mechanism is believed to have diverged from the parent Ang IV / IRAP biology but this has not been structurally established.
expanded.safetyDetail: No human safety data. Rodent studies have not reported major toxicity at tested doses but are not substitutes for human exposure characterization. Broad c-Met activation is a theoretical oncological concern — c-Met is a known oncogene in several cancer types (gastric, lung, renal) and is a target of several approved oncology drugs acting as c-Met inhibitors. A compound that augments HGF/c-Met signaling runs directly contrary to that oncology drug-development logic.

safetyRisks.label: c-Met pathway activation
safetyRisks.helpSummary: c-Met is an established oncogene target.
safetyRisks.harmSummary: c-Met gain-of-function is causally implicated in multiple cancers, and c-Met inhibitors are an approved oncology class. A compound that augments HGF/c-Met signaling carries a real oncology concern, particularly in individuals with occult malignancy or cancer risk factors.

safetyRisks.label: No human safety characterization
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Gray-market use lacks any clinical trial foundation.

safetyRisks.label: Extraordinary potency claims require extraordinary replication
safetyRisks.harmSummary: The 'orders of magnitude more potent than BDNF' framing is striking and not robustly replicated by independent groups at the originally reported magnitude.

safetyRisks.label: Unverified gray-market identity
safetyRisks.harmSummary: Dihexa's non-standard peptidomimetic structure makes identity verification by standard peptide-synthesis QC less straightforward than for canonical peptides; gray-market material should be assumed unverified.

enhancementPotential.summary: Cognitive enhancement claims in healthy adults are not supported by any human trial. Rodent data are suggestive but single-source at the claimed potency magnitude.
enhancementPotential.caveat: c-Met is an oncogene and an established oncology drug target (for inhibitors, not activators). A cognitive-enhancing compound that activates this pathway is not a casual proposition in healthy adults.

missingEvidence: Any human clinical trial
Independent replication of the high-potency synaptogenesis claim by unaffiliated laboratories
Long-term oncological safety given c-Met pathway engagement
Validated human pharmacokinetics and bioavailability
Verified molecular identity in gray-market supply
anecdotalUse: Gray-market oral or sublingual use for claimed cognitive enhancement and neuroprotection — not supported by any human trial

claims.value: Dihexa is proposed to act through HGF / c-Met pathway augmentation, with preclinical evidence for dendritic spine formation and behavioral improvement in rodent models; direct binding to HGF or c-Met has not been convincingly established.
claims.population: rat behavioral models; cultured hippocampal neurons

claims.value: The reported synaptogenic potency of dihexa — 'orders of magnitude greater than BDNF' — has not been robustly replicated at that magnitude by independent groups; the combination of extraordinary claim and single-lineage evidence warrants independent verification.
c-Met is an established oncogene and oncology drug target (for inhibitors); a cognitive-enhancing compound that activates HGF / c-Met signaling carries a concrete theoretical oncology concern.
No published human clinical trials of dihexa exist as of knowledge cutoff.

citations.title: Facilitation of hippocampal synaptogenesis and spatial memory by C-terminal truncated Nle1-angiotensin IV analogs
citations.notes: J Pharmacol Exp Ther 339(1):35-44. Harding-lab paper on Ang IV-derived analog class including design rationale; dihexa-specific characterization is split across several Harding-lab papers from 2011-2014.

citations.title: Evaluation of metabolically stabilized angiotensin IV analogs as pro-cognitive / anti-dementia therapeutics
citations.notes: J Pharmacol Exp Ther 344(1):141-54. Further Harding-lab characterization; confirm exact title and year.
